title: Asbestos Fest

jokes.text: I had to be both puppet, and puppeteer.
jokes.setup: Eva Longoria dropped out of their ventriloquist act benefit
jokes.punchline: Moira's dramatic declaration of having to perform both roles
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Moira's self-important delusion and dramatic flair. The absurdity of the situation combined with her delivery makes this memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I'll just have to be both the ventriloquist AND the dummy.

jokes.text: Shoes, Glorious Shoes, the Imelda Marcos Story
jokes.setup: Moira needs to choose what to perform
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and inappropriate musical choice
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific character comedy. The idea of turning Imelda Marcos into a musical about shoes is perfectly absurd and very Moira.

jokes.text: Two Heads are Better. The one woman Siamese twin play.
jokes.setup: Moira deciding between performance options
jokes.punchline: The contradiction of a one-woman Siamese twin play
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist comedy. The logical impossibility delivered with Moira's complete seriousness makes this a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A one-woman Siamese twin play. Now that's a contradiction.

jokes.text: Okay Moira, I just wanna slap you right across the face. Patty it is! Patty.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn's mounting frustration with Moira's indecision
jokes.punchline: Blunt threat followed by immediate capitulation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jocelyn's exasperation, the bluntness is funny

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Moira, if you don't make a decision right now, I'm going to lose my mind.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Actually, no, take your time. Whatever works for you.

jokes.text: does 'TV's Moira Rose' send a different message than 'television's Moira Rose?'
jokes.setup: Moira obsessing over billing details
jokes.punchline: The completely meaningless distinction she's concerned about
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Moira's self-absorption and delusions of grandeur about meaningless distinctions.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need to know if I'm being billed as 'Moira Rose' or 'Ms. Moira Rose.' They are two completely different people.

jokes.text: Well, didn't it have an ensemble cast of 30? Uh huh, and what was your father's review? 29 dead weights!
jokes.setup: David questioning the large cast size
jokes.punchline: Johnny's brutal review calling 29 of 30 cast members dead weights
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup-punchline with perfect economy. Johnny's savage review is both shocking and character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why does this production need 30 people?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David, it's a musical. You need dancers, singers, ensemble members—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but 30?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just read the review. He said 29 of the 30 cast members were dead weights.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, which one did he like?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: He didn't say.

jokes.text: The night Patricia Lupone ate that pre-show shwarma, and I was asked to step in.
jokes.setup: David questioning if Moira even played Patty Hearst
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific circumstance of her one performance
jokes.explanation: Great specificity in the absurd detail. The idea of Patricia LuPone being derailed by shawarma is wonderfully random yet believable in Moira's world.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you even play Patty Hearst?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I did play Patty Hearst. It was a made-for-TV movie that aired at 2 AM on a Tuesday in April, during a cable blackout in the greater Tri-State area.

jokes.text: you practicing every day for seven months with that dance instructor that I ended up dating
jokes.setup: David recalling Moira's preparation for the show
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that he dated her dance instructor
jokes.explanation: Perfect David moment - the casual drop of inappropriate personal information that makes everyone uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She hired a dance instructor to prepare for the show. Turns out, I dated him.

jokes.text: Skip Fosse once told me that when it comes to choreo, always leave some room for spontaneity.
jokes.setup: Moira justifying her approach to choreography
jokes.punchline: Name-dropping 'Skip Fosse' (implying she knew Bob Fosse but got his name wrong)
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira name-drop gone wrong - 'Skip Fosse' instead of Bob Fosse shows her pretentious but inaccurate memory

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I studied under Skip Fosse.

jokes.text: No, you say, 'break a leg.' - Okay, that, too. - Say 'break a leg!' Break a leg!
jokes.setup: David casually wishing her luck
jokes.punchline: Moira's insistent correction and David's reluctant compliance
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy showing Moira's need for theatrical tradition and David's resignation

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Good luck.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Break a leg.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's what I said.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, you said 'good luck.' You say 'break a leg' to someone in the theater.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Break a leg, Mother.

jokes.text: We're going to ease in... with a machine gun ballet.
jokes.setup: Moira beginning her rehearsal
jokes.punchline: The contradiction of 'easing in' with machine gun ballet
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of 'ease in' with 'machine gun ballet' - perfectly captures Moira's delusions about her theatrical choices.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The contradiction of 'easing in' with machine gun ballet

jokes.text: there was one that looked meth-y. And I don't know where he went.
jokes.setup: Patrick saying the teens scare off customers
jokes.punchline: David's casual observation about a potentially dangerous customer
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor that shows David's sheltered perspective on actual danger.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, at least they're scaring off the potentially dangerous customers.

jokes.text: The fact that we have youths minding their own business in front of our store builds... Street cred.
jokes.setup: Arguing about teens hanging around the store
jokes.punchline: David's earnest belief that loitering teens give them street credibility
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's disconnect from business reality and his need to feel cool.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're not loitering, they're just... lingering. And frankly, I think it gives us a certain street credibility.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, we sell high-end home furnishings in a small town. We don't need street credibility.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, maybe that's why we're not thriving.

jokes.text: Yes! They're coming in, and they're buying gum. And what else are they doing, David? They're coming in, they're looking around, and they're buying gum. And maybe complimenting my outfits.
jokes.setup: Listing what the teens actually buy
jokes.punchline: The repetition revealing it's just gum, plus the vain addition about compliments
jokes.explanation: The repetition emphasizes how little they're buying, and David's vanity about compliments is in character

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, let's see what the teens are actually purchasing. Gum. Gum. Gum. Gum. And apparently, compliments about my bone structure.

jokes.text: This is so old, and I can't even remember where I got it. Do you guys remember-sorry, where I got this sweater?
jokes.setup: Teen compliments David's sweater
jokes.punchline: David's desperate attempt to seem casual while seeking validation
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing David's desperation for teen approval manifesting in awkward over-sharing.

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: That's a really nice sweater.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This? Oh, it's just something I threw on. It's vintage Loro Piana. I got it in Milan. Well, I didn't get it myself, my stylist got it, but I was there. Spiritually, I was very much involved in the process.

jokes.text: What just happened to you? Like, breathe it out.
jokes.setup: David's awkward interaction with the teens
jokes.punchline: Patrick's concerned reaction to David's obvious desperation
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat highlighting David's obvious desperation through Patrick's concerned response.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, are you okay? You're being weird.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being weird. Why would you say that?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Because you just tried to high-five a fourteen-year-old and called TikTok 'very now.'

jokes.text: Roland, what are you talking about? Well, you are gonna be thanking me pretty soon for saving your bacon, so I just thought I'd give you some gift ideas.
jokes.setup: Roland making a confusing statement about flowers and beer
jokes.punchline: His presumptuous suggestion of gift ideas for thanking him for something he hasn't done
jokes.explanation: Roland's presumptuous nature comes through in expecting gratitude gifts before doing anything helpful

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I was just thinking, when you want to thank me for helping you with your business, you could get me some flowers and a case of beer.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I haven't asked you to help me with anything.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know, but when you do, I like tulips.

jokes.text: And I'm sorry, but we all know how Rose Video turned out!
jokes.setup: Johnny defending his business experience with Rose Video
jokes.punchline: Roland's brutal reminder of the business failure
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that deflates Johnny's pompousness with a harsh reality check delivered in Roland's blunt style.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I ran a successful business for years.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You ran it into the ground.

jokes.text: Four teens, one pack of gum, which you gift wrapped.
jokes.setup: David celebrating a sale
jokes.punchline: The absurd inefficiency of gift-wrapping gum for teens
jokes.explanation: Good observational comedy highlighting the absurd economics of David's customer service approach.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just sold a extremely rare piece to a very important client.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's amazing! We should celebrate. What do you want to do?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, something special. Maybe we could get gift-wrapped gum for teenagers?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Gift-wrapped gum? For teenagers?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Think about it — you spend twenty minutes carefully wrapping a stick of gum, only for them to unwrap it in three seconds and immediately throw away the paper. It's the perfect metaphor for inefficiency and wasted effort.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... that's not a celebration, David. That's a cry for help.

jokes.text: Four teens, one pack of gum, which you gift wrapped.
jokes.setup: Commenting on the poor sales ratio
jokes.punchline: The absurd detail that David gift wrapped a single pack of gum
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail - only David would gift wrap a pack of gum, showing his over-the-top customer service

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David gift wrapped a single pack of gum.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was a nice pack of gum.

jokes.text: It's the exact same move that I used with my klepto friends in the Hamptons.
jokes.setup: Patrick explaining the shoplifting pattern
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of his own criminal past in upscale settings
jokes.explanation: Great character revelation showing Patrick's unexpected criminal sophistication, delivered with perfect casualness.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I used to shoplift from high-end boutiques in the Hamptons.

jokes.text: Yeah, I mean, maybe that's what you and your burnout friends did at the Miss Sixty store, but that's not what's happening here.
jokes.setup: David defending against Alexis's shoplifting theory
jokes.punchline: Ironically dismissing her experience while she's clearly right
jokes.explanation: David's denial while the evidence is mounting, plus the specific reference to Miss Sixty

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not a shoplifter, Alexis. I have a very good relationship with retail establishments.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you literally got caught stealing a cashmere sweater last week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was a misunderstanding. The tag fell off.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You cut it off.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, regardless, I am not a shoplifter.

jokes.text: maybe that's what you and your burnout friends did at the Miss Sixty store
jokes.setup: David defending against shoplifting accusation
jokes.punchline: His defensive specificity about fashion retail reveals his own experience
jokes.explanation: Good defensive character moment with brand specificity that suggests familiarity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would never shoplift. Do you know how mortifying it would be to be caught stealing from a store? The humiliation, the police involvement, the permanent record... I'm very familiar with retail loss prevention protocols, and I can assure you, the cameras are very thorough. They cover every angle of the store, including the fitting rooms and the stockroom. I know this because I've worked in fashion retail, and I take these matters very seriously.

jokes.text: if they're not selling it on the black market, then they'll need to come back and get the moisturizer, or their t-zones are going to be like, super effed!
jokes.setup: Explaining why the teens will return
jokes.punchline: Patrick's expertise in both crime and skincare routines
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy combining Patrick's criminal knowledge with unexpectedly detailed skincare expertise.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, the teens will return because I have expertise in both crime and skincare routines.

jokes.text: Johnny, it's me! I'm the guy! I know you're the guy, I got that as soon as I said where's the guy?
jokes.setup: Roland's dramatic revelation that he's the guy Johnny needs
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate deflation that he figured it out immediately
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Roland's dramatic moment with Johnny's practical response

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm the guy you need.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yeah, I figured that out immediately.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's probably a good idea, I don't wanna commit until I know I like it.
jokes.setup: Johnny saying it's a trial employment
jokes.punchline: Roland's oblivious response treating it like he's doing them a favor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland moment showing his complete lack of self-awareness about power dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a trial employment.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I'm doing you a favor then.

jokes.text: So I need a machine gun for the machine gun ballet!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn brings a hunting rifle as prop
jokes.punchline: Moira's indignant insistence on the specific weapon type
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier machine gun ballet line, showing Moira's commitment to her absurd artistic vision.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's not a hunting rifle, it's a carbine. There's a difference.

jokes.text: On my wedding day, I thought, what if I just got in my car and drove to New Mexico, and left Roland at the altar at the Elmdale Bingo Hall?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn trying to encourage Moira about facing fears
jokes.punchline: The revelation of her wedding day panic and the sad venue
jokes.explanation: Great character moment revealing Jocelyn's past doubts while the Bingo Hall detail adds perfect small-town sadness.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You know, Moira, facing your fears is really important. I had to face mine at my wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh really? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I panicked. Completely panicked. I thought I was going to faint.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I got through it. And you know what? The venue was a Sandals Jamaica.

jokes.text: I got these at a boutique in Prague that's only open on Sunday nights.
jokes.setup: Teen asking about David's shoes
jokes.punchline: David's ridiculously specific and pretentious origin story
jokes.explanation: Perfect David character moment - unnecessarily elaborate and pretentious backstory for shoes. 'Only open on Sunday nights' is wonderfully specific.

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: Those are nice shoes. Where'd you get them?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These are Italian leather, hand-stitched by a cobbler in Milan who only takes on three clients per year. I had to wait eighteen months for an appointment, and he insisted on measuring my feet at dawn because he believed that's when the foot is at its most honest. The leather was sourced from a specific region in Tuscany where the cows are apparently very calm, which affects the quality of the hide. They cost more than your car.

jokes.text: I got these at a boutique in Prague that's only open on Sunday nights.
jokes.setup: Teen asking about David's shoes
jokes.punchline: David's completely over-the-top fake origin story
jokes.explanation: Perfect David pretentiousness - the specificity of 'Sunday nights only' makes the lie absurdly elaborate.

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: Those are nice shoes. Where'd you get them?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. These are vintage Italian leather. I acquired them in Milan during a particularly challenging time in my life. I was there for a spring collection preview, and I simply saw them in a window and knew. The cobbler— his name was Giuseppe, very temperamental— he actually refused to sell them to me at first because he said they were meant for someone special. But I convinced him otherwise.

jokes.text: someone with your combination of skin should really be using product with tea tree oil in it
jokes.setup: Patrick confronting the shoplifting teen
jokes.punchline: Offering skincare advice while catching them stealing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Patrick's unexpected expertise and tendency to be helpful even when confronting criminals.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Excuse me, I noticed you putting that moisturizer in your pocket. Now, I'm going to have to ask you to put that back, but first — and I say this with genuine concern — your skin is incredibly dehydrated. You really should be using a hydrating serum before your moisturizer. It makes all the difference.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So yes, put it back, but also, come find me when you're ready to start a proper skincare routine. We can set you up with something that will actually work for your skin type.

jokes.text: Don't do it again, and I won't call the Po Po.
jokes.setup: Patrick threatening to call police
jokes.punchline: His use of the childish term 'Po Po'
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy showing Patrick's attempts to be intimidating while using childish slang.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm going to call the po po.

jokes.text: Don't do it again, and I won't call the Po Po. - Did she say Po Po?
jokes.setup: Alexis threatening to call police
jokes.punchline: Using 'Po Po' and the teen's confused reaction
jokes.explanation: Alexis using outdated slang while trying to be tough, and the teen's genuine confusion

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm calling the Po Po!

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: The what?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The Po Po. The police.

dialogue.speaker: Teen
dialogue.line: ...Did you just say 'Po Po'?

jokes.text: Well Johnny, how's it going to look to have the mayor of the town changing sheets?
jokes.setup: Johnny asking Roland to change sheets
jokes.punchline: Roland's inflated sense of his mayoral importance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Roland's delusions about his status in a tiny town.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, could you please change the sheets?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Change the sheets? Do you know who I am? I'm the mayor of this town. I don't change sheets. I change policy.

jokes.text: And Jocelyn had to sell her Beanie Baby collection. And she didn't get half what that Diana bear was worth.
jokes.setup: Roland explaining their financial hardship
jokes.punchline: The specific mention of the Diana bear's undervaluation
jokes.explanation: Nice period-specific detail about 90s collectibles and the specific sadness of the Diana bear investment.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We lost everything. The house, the business, the Diana bear... do you know what that bear is worth? Nothing. Absolutely nothing. I paid forty thousand dollars for it.

jokes.text: There is an elephant in the room David, and he's whispering, 'retire!'
jokes.setup: Moira admitting defeat about her performance
jokes.punchline: The dramatic personification of retirement as a whispering elephant
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Moira dialogue combining her theatrical nature with absurd metaphor. The whispering elephant is perfectly bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I suppose I must accept that my time in the spotlight has dimmed. Like a whispering elephant in the darkness of retirement, slowly fading into the distance.

jokes.text: I will eventually need my bed back.
jokes.setup: David explaining why he'll help Moira with the performance
jokes.punchline: The practical, selfish motivation delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect David moment - cutting through sentiment with practical self-interest delivered with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if you don't do well, you'll be insufferable for months, and I have to live with you.

jokes.text: even though somebody put down a really hefty deposit on a wall of mirrors!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn introducing the last-minute change to Moira's act
jokes.punchline: The expensive mirror setup that's now useless
jokes.explanation: Shows the elaborate preparation that went into Moira's original act, now wasted

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So, um, we've had a last-minute change to Moira's act.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What kind of change?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, the magician cancelled, so we're going to need you to do something else.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But I've already had the expensive mirror setup installed!

jokes.text: It's the middle of summer. Brr! It's awfully cold out there.
jokes.setup: David and Moira performing a Christmas number in summer
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's confused reaction to the seasonal mismatch
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about the obvious seasonal inappropriateness of the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But... it's July.

jokes.text: Um, they used to do this act every year at our Christmas party, and you can't unsee something like this.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explaining the history of David and Moira's act
jokes.punchline: Warning the audience they can't unsee what's about to happen
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for what's coming - warning the audience about the traumatic nature of the performance

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I have to warn you, what you're about to see, you cannot unsee.

jokes.text: That's television's mom, to you.
jokes.setup: Moira introducing herself as 'television's Moira Rose'
jokes.punchline: David's correction asserting their relationship
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows David asserting their relationship while playing along with Moira's performance persona.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's my mother.

jokes.text: nothing is colder than the chill I get when I think of the dangers of asbestos poisoning
jokes.setup: Winter-themed performance about being cold
jokes.punchline: Connecting cold to asbestos poisoning for the fundraiser
jokes.explanation: Good transition connecting the winter theme to the fundraiser cause, delivered in Moira's theatrical style.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm performing a winter-themed number about the bitter cold. It's a fundraiser, you see, and I've decided to connect the numbing sensation of frigid temperatures to the slow, agonizing effects of asbestos poisoning.

jokes.text: Yes, I said faithful, which rules out all of you!
jokes.setup: Christmas song performance about being faithful
jokes.punchline: David's snarky aside insulting the audience
jokes.explanation: Good David moment showing his inability to resist making cutting comments even during a performance.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's enough of that. I can see some of you are already checking your phones, which is fine because clearly, you have the emotional capacity of a napkin.

tmdb_overview: Moira prepares for her headline debut at the annual Schitt's Creek Asbestos Fest, while David deals with a group of loitering teens in the apothecary.
insight: Festival planning chaos yields 43 jokes in 43 minutes, grounded in character spite.
